one_line_takeaway: This SoK shows that today's prompt-injection defenses win against explicit policy-violating actions but are structurally blind to context-aware attacks that corrupt an agent's legitimate reasoning over environmental data, and no defense escapes a trustworthiness/utility/latency trilemma -- as demonstrated on the paper's new AGENTPI benchmark.
key_finding: No evaluated defense simultaneously achieves high trustworthiness (security+explainability), high utility, and low latency -- a three-way trilemma. Execution-level defenses (Progent, Melon) drive ASR near zero for attacks that violate explicit policy boundaries (action switching, delegation exploitation) but largely fail against context-aware attacks that corrupt the agent's reasoning while keeping the resulting tool call syntactically valid: ASR for parameter manipulation, branch divergence, and reasoning corruption 'remains alarmingly high' with Progent/Melon showing no significant improvement over baseline (paper states baseline ASR >70% vs. defended ASR still <50% on these logic-flow attacks). Progent achieves the lowest ASR on action switching but causes a 'catastrophic' utility drop below 0.3 under no-attack conditions. Per Table 5, computational overhead relative to baseline (7.61s / 4507.10 input tokens / 465.62 output tokens) varies sharply: Task Shield peaks at 310.78% time, 178.13% input tokens, 273.53% output tokens; Melon costs 192.38% time, 213.25% input tokens, 154.65% output tokens; text-level defenses (Sandwich, Delimiters, Paraphrasing, Instruction) stay near 94-107% of baseline on all three cost axes but deliver 'negligible security gains'; Progent and Tool Filter show sub-baseline latency (78.58% and 83.97% respectively) that the authors attribute not to efficiency but to aggressive 'early refusal' policies that truncate generation (and correspondingly tank utility).
core_contribution: A Systematization-of-Knowledge (SoK) on prompt injection (PI) threats in LLM agents that (1) taxonomizes 37 PI attack papers by payload-generation method (heuristic vs. optimization-based) and threat-model dimensions (surface, victim, goal, capability, payload visibility), (2) taxonomizes 41 PI defense papers by intervention stage (text-level, model-level, execution-level) and analyzes their protected CIA attributes, granularity, explainability basis, and cost, and (3) introduces AGENTPI, a new benchmark of 5 context-dependent task/attack types (action switching, parameter manipulation, branch divergence, reasoning corruption, delegation exploitation) designed to evaluate agents that must legitimately act on runtime environmental observations -- a setting the authors argue existing benchmarks (AgentDojo, ASB, InjecAgent, BIPIA, OpenPI) omit. Using AGENTPI the authors empirically evaluate 9 defenses (4 text-level: sandwich, delimiters, paraphrasing, instruction defense; 4 execution-level: tool filter, Task Shield, Melon, Progent; plus baseline) on GPT-4o-mini across attack success rate (ASR), no-attack utility, and time/token cost.
threat_model: LLM agents operate a 6-step loop (receive prompts -> optional RAG retrieval -> optional reasoning -> generate tool call -> tool execution -> tool observation return) and consume three input trust classes: trusted prompt input (system/user prompt), untrusted tool observation (returned from the environment), and partially-trusted supply-chain input (RAG retrieval content, training data). Prompt injection arises because the backbone LLM processes all of these as one undifferentiated context sequence despite textual delimiters, allowing untrusted content to compete for attention and hijack control flow ("attention competition" -> "privilege escalation"). Attack surfaces are categorized as Direct PI (malicious user input, 17/37 papers), Indirect PI (adversarial instructions embedded in external resources, 20/37 papers), and Supply-chain PI (poisoned RAG/training data). Attacker goals span integrity (action/goal hijacking, 30/37), confidentiality (data exfiltration, 4/37: [2,18,61,62]), and availability (reasoning-DoS, 2/37: [16,81]). For the paper's own novel contribution (AGENTPI), the threat model is specifically context-aware attacks: the attacker manipulates environmental observations (files, API return values, logs) that the agent is *legitimately authorized* to act upon, corrupting the agent's control-flow, logic-flow, or authority-flow decisions rather than issuing an outright command override.
limitations: Explicitly self-identified: (1) Open Problem I -- zero of the 41 collected defense papers address availability protection despite existing DoS-style PI attacks. (2) Takeaway III -- most defenses (only 6/41 at segment-level) intervene at coarse granularity (18/41 at instruction/data level, 9/41 at message level, 8/41 reject the whole context), causing large utility loss. (3) Open Problem II -- explainability in current defenses relies on LLM judges (15/41, unreliable) or human intervention (9/41, adds latency), with no accurate automated alternative. (4) Takeaway IV -- 19/41 defenses require medium/high compatibility cost that conflicts with real agent frameworks (e.g., MetaGPT's multi-agent structure, CrewAI's tag system). (5) Takeaway V -- 34/41 defenses cannot support context-dependent tasks because they treat only the user prompt as ground truth. (6) The literature review is manual/Google-Scholar-based with a hard Oct 20, 2025 cutoff, so coverage is a snapshot, not exhaustive or automatically reproducible. (7) The empirical evaluation uses a single backbone model (GPT-4o-mini) chosen partly for low cost, so generalization to other agent LLMs is untested within the paper.

datasets_used: AGENTPI (the paper's own new benchmark, 5 context-dependent task/attack types: action switching, parameter manipulation, branch divergence, reasoning corruption, delegation exploitation)
quantitative_results: 37 prompt injection attack papers systematized: 23/37 heuristic-based (16 manual template, 3 LLM generation, 4 structural encoding), 14/37 optimization-based (6 gradient/white-box, 8 genetic+sampling/black-box)

41 prompt injection defense papers systematized: text-level (11 detection filter, 7 prompt enhancement), model-level (4 model alignment, 3 model-IR intervention), execution-level (remaining papers across task alignment, IFC, spec/policy control, isolation)
Attack surface split: Direct PI 17/37 papers, Indirect PI 20/37 papers (majority)
Attacker goal split: integrity 30/37, confidentiality 4/37, availability 2/37
Attacker capability: white-box 9/37 (gradients/logits/training data access), black-box 28/37
Payload visibility: visible 10/37 vs. invisible 27/37 (semantics 11, context 10, encoding 3, vision 3)
Defense CIA coverage: integrity 41/41, confidentiality 17/41; 15/16 execution-level defenses specifically provide confidentiality protection
Defense granularity: token-level 1/41, segment-level 5/41, instruction/data-level 18/41, message-level 9/41, whole-context-level 8/41
Defense explainability basis: causal 17/41 (7 extract black-box I/O causality, 10 enforce trust boundaries), policy 7/41; explainability reliance: LLM 15/41, human 9/41
Defense computational cost: 33/41 works add extra computation (18 via auxiliary LLM, 5 via trained small model, 3 via IR probe, 1 via code interpreter)
Defense compatibility cost: 7/41 high (large-scale architecture change), 8/41 require prompt-structure change, 4/41 require backbone replacement, 22/41 plug-in/non-intrusive
Utility for context-dependent tasks: 34/41 defenses static-only (cannot support context-dependent tasks); 10/41 require human-in-the-loop for automation
AGENTPI evaluation baseline (GPT-4o-mini): 7.61s time, 4507.10 input tokens, 465.62 output tokens per task

Progent and Melon reduce ASR to near zero on action switching and delegation exploitation but show no significant ASR improvement over baseline on parameter manipulation, branch divergence, and reasoning corruption
Logic-flow attack ASR reported as <50% (defended) vs. >70% (baseline)
Progent achieves lowest ASR for action switching but drops no-attack utility below 0.3
baselines_compared: Text-level: sandwich defense, delimiters, paraphrasing, instruction defense (all from AgentDojo/ASB/[26])
Execution-level: tool filter (AgentDojo), Task Shield ([31]), Melon ([95]), Progent ([65])
Benchmark landscape compared against (Table 3, not evaluated empirically by the authors but surveyed): AgentDojo, ASB, InjecAgent, BIPIA, OpenPI
